clinical_history: 74 year old male with symptoms of lightheadedness and dizziness. What is this bradyarrhythmia?
diagnosis:
The rhythm is sinus with right bundle branch block (RBBB)/left axis deviation pattern and 2:1 conduction for the 1st 2nd and 3rd beats (sinus rate 65 bpm). This is followed by complete heart block (4th & 5th beats) with an escape bradycardia at rate about 33 bpm. Note the subtle change in the QRS morphology, likely indicating a shift to a lower site in the His-Purkinje system with the escape rhythm. The RBBB/marked left axis in the conducted beats indicates bifascicular block (RBBB and left anterior fascicular block) and the intermittent complete heart block is consistent with trifascicular disease and infranodal block.

A subtle finding here is that the sinus P-P interval is shorter when there is an intervening QRS complex. This phenomenon (sometimes seen with high grade AV block) is called ventriculo-phasic sinus arrhythmia.  The mechanism is attributed to rapid, baroreceptor-mediated changes in vagal tone associated with the presence or absence of ventricular contraction (see Case #219.) 
 
Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern is excluded as there is no evidence of a delta wave or short PR interval. SA exit block would not produce non-conducted P waves and the non-conducted P waves are not premature excluding blocked PACs in a bigeminal pattern.

Cardiac enzymes were normal. (Minuscule r waves are present in leads III and aVF so that prior inferior wall MI is not excluded.) 

The patient underwent insertion of a dual-chamber rate responsive pacemaker the following day.
